subject: ap-chemistry
title: HBr vs HF: Intermolecular Forces & Vaporization
stimulus: Answer the following questions related to HBr(l) and HF(l).

parts.prompt:
In the following table, list all of the types of intermolecular forces present in pure samples of HBr(l) and HF(l).

| Liquid | HBr(l) | HF(l) |
|---|---|---|
| Intermolecular forces present | | |
parts.scoring_notes: HBr(l): London dispersion forces (LDF) and dipole-dipole interactions. (HBr has a polar H–Br bond but does not qualify for H-bonding because Br is not N, O, or F.) HF(l): London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions, AND hydrogen bonding (H directly bonded to F, the most electronegative atom — classic H-bond donor/acceptor).

scoring_points.description: For the correct complete list of IMFs for both HBr and HF: 1 point

required_elements: HBr: London dispersion forces AND dipole-dipole interactions (no H-bonding).
HF: London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions, AND hydrogen bonding.
alternatives.correct_example:
HBr(l): London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions.
HF(l): London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions, hydrogen bonding.

HBr does not form H-bonds because Br is not sufficiently electronegative (H-bonding requires H bonded to N, O, or F). HF qualifies because H is bonded to F.

wrong_examples: Including hydrogen bonding for HBr — Br is not one of the three atoms (N, O, F) that engage in classical H-bonding.
Omitting London dispersion forces for either — all molecules have LDFs (induced dipole interactions).
common_traps: Hydrogen bonding occurs ONLY when H is directly bonded to N, O, or F. Br is too large and not electronegative enough for H-bonding. Both polar molecules have dipole-dipole forces AND London dispersion forces.

parts.prompt:
The enthalpy of vaporization, $\Delta H_{vap}$, for each liquid is provided in the following table.

| Liquid | HBr(l) | HF(l) |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H_{vap}$ | 17.3 kJ/mol | 25.2 kJ/mol |

(i) Based on the types and relative strengths of intermolecular forces, explain why $\Delta H_{vap}$ of HF(l) is greater than that of HBr(l).

(ii) Calculate the amount of thermal energy, in kJ, required to vaporize 6.85 g of HF(l).
parts.scoring_notes: (i) HF has hydrogen bonding in addition to LDF and dipole-dipole forces. HBr only has LDF and dipole-dipole forces. Hydrogen bonds are stronger IMFs than dipole-dipole or LDF alone. More energy is required to overcome the stronger/more IMFs in HF → higher $\Delta H_{vap}$. (ii) Molar mass of HF = 1.01 + 19.00 = 20.01 g/mol. $n = 6.85/20.01 = 0.342$ mol. $q = 0.342 \times 25.2 = 8.62$ kJ.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing hydrogen bonding as the reason HF has higher $\Delta H_{vap}$: 1 point

required_elements: HF has hydrogen bonding (in addition to LDF and dipole-dipole), which is stronger than the IMFs in HBr.
More energy is needed to vaporize HF because stronger IMFs must be overcome.
alternatives.correct_example: HF has hydrogen bonding in addition to London dispersion and dipole-dipole forces. HBr has only London dispersion and dipole-dipole forces. Hydrogen bonds are stronger intermolecular forces than dipole-dipole interactions. Because the intermolecular forces in HF(l) are stronger, more energy is required to separate the molecules during vaporization, resulting in a higher $\Delta H_{vap}$ for HF.

wrong_examples: Saying HBr has stronger LDFs due to more electrons — while HBr has more electrons (LDF is larger), the H-bonding in HF more than compensates, which is why $\Delta H_{vap}$(HF) > $\Delta H_{vap}$(HBr).
Citing only polarity without naming H-bonding — must specifically name H-bonding as the key additional force in HF.
common_traps: Even though HBr has more electrons (larger LDF), HF's hydrogen bonds are so strong that they dominate. The data confirms HF has higher $\Delta H_{vap}$. Answer must cite H-bonding in HF and relate it to higher energy needed for vaporization.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated thermal energy in kJ: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{HF}) = \dfrac{6.85\text{ g}}{20.01\text{ g/mol}} = 0.342\text{ mol}$
$q = 0.342\text{ mol} \times 25.2\text{ kJ/mol} = 8.62\text{ kJ}$

wrong_examples: Using molar mass of HBr (80.91 g/mol) instead of HF (20.01 g/mol).
Multiplying mass directly by $\Delta H_{vap}$ without converting to moles first.
common_traps: $q = n \times \Delta H_{vap}$. Molar mass of HF = 1.01 + 19.00 = 20.01 g/mol. Convert grams to moles; multiply by $\Delta H_{vap}$ in kJ/mol.

parts.prompt: Based on the arrangement of electrons in the Br and F atoms, explain why the bond length in an HBr molecule is greater than that in an HF molecule.
parts.scoring_notes: Br has atomic number 35 and has electrons in n = 1, 2, 3, and 4 principal energy levels. F has atomic number 9 and has electrons only in n = 1 and 2. Because Br has additional occupied electron shells (n = 3 and n = 4) beyond those of F, the valence electrons of Br are at a greater average distance from the nucleus. When Br forms a bond with H, the bonding pair is farther from the Br nucleus than in HF, resulting in a longer H–Br bond compared to H–F.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing additional occupied electron shells in Br vs. F: 1 point

required_elements: Br has more occupied electron shells (up to n = 4) than F (up to n = 2).
The greater number of electron shells places the bonding electrons farther from the Br nucleus, leading to a longer bond in HBr compared to HF.
alternatives.correct_example: The Br atom has electrons in four principal energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3, 4), while the F atom has electrons in only two (n = 1, 2). The additional occupied shells in Br increase the average distance of the bonding pair from the Br nucleus compared to the H–F bond. This larger atomic radius of Br compared to F results in a longer H–Br bond length.

wrong_examples: Citing only that Br is 'bigger' without explaining why — must reference the number of occupied principal quantum levels.
Saying Br has more protons — Br (Z=35) has more protons than F (Z=9), but the shielding from additional shells increases the effective atomic radius more than the nuclear charge decreases it.
common_traps: Bond length ∝ atomic radius. Atomic radius increases down a group due to additional occupied principal energy levels (electron shells). More shells = more shielding = electrons further from nucleus = larger atom = longer bond.
